mappings.rationaleAI: The origins of the universe from the Big Bang through inflation theory addresses relativistic cosmologies (A.1.f.iv), cosmic background radiation (A.1.f.vii), and astrophysics/cosmology (B.5).
Black holes from Einstein's general relativity through Hawking radiation address identification of possible black holes (A.1.f.vii), development of relativity (A.2.e.i), and astrophysics (B.5).

Comets from ancient omens through Halley's periodicity prediction to Rosetta address history of astronomy from ancient through modern (A.1.c.i, A.1.f.v) and component disciplines including solar system study (B.2).

Maxwell's unification of electricity, magnetism, and light addresses development of electromagnetic theories (A.2.d.ii), quantum mechanics connections (A.2.e.ii), and the component disciplines of physics (C.2).
Pauli's Exclusion Principle explains the periodic table structure and stability of matter, connecting to atomic structure theories (A.2.e.iii) and the nature of chemistry (D.1).
The second law of thermodynamics from Carnot through Boltzmann's statistical mechanics covers the development of thermodynamic theories (A.2.d.iii) and a core discipline of physics (C.2).
Alchemy from Hellenistic Egypt through Islamic civilization to early modern Europe covers chemistry before 1700 (A.3.a) including the alchemists' goals of transmutation (A.3.a.i).

The history of chemical elements from Greek concepts through Mendeleev's periodic table covers laboratory discoveries (A.3.b.ii), development of the periodic table (A.3.c.i), and the nature of chemistry (D.1).
The history of heat from caloric theory to thermodynamics directly covers development of thermodynamic theories (A.2.d.iii) and a core discipline of physics (C.2).
Electromagnetic radiation from Maxwell through X-rays and radioactivity covers development of electromagnetic wave theories (A.2.d.ii) and the discovery of radioactivity and X-rays (A.2.d.iv).
Einstein's contributions to relativity (A.2.e.i), quantum theory (A.2.e.ii), and the 1919 eclipse confirmation as an astronomical test of general relativity (A.1.f.iii).
Exoplanet discovery and detection methods represent major advances in solar-system and planetary astronomy (A.1.f.v) and component disciplines of astronomy (B.2).
Galaxy classification, formation, evolution, and supermassive black holes address statistical studies of galaxies (A.1.f.i) and astrophysics (B.5).
Quantum gravity attempts to reconcile quantum mechanics and general relativity, addressing unified field theory efforts (A.2.e.iii) and philosophical problems in physics (C.6).
The age of the universe from Hubble's expanding universe through cosmic microwave background measurements addresses relativistic cosmologies (A.1.f.iv) and astrophysics/cosmology (B.5).
The death of stars covering supernovae, neutron stars, black holes, and nucleosynthesis addresses theories of stellar evolution (A.1.f.ii) and astrophysics (B.5).
The electron's discovery by Thomson and its quantum behavior address discovery of the electron (A.2.d.iv), quantum theory development (A.2.e.ii), and atomic structure (A.2.e.iii).

The history of optics from Greek theories through Newton's experiments to quantum optics covers Greek physics (A.2.a), 16th-17th century physics (A.2.c), the wave-corpuscular debate (A.2.d.i), and physics disciplines (C.2).
Oxygen's discovery and Lavoisier's dismantling of phlogiston theory cover 18th-century combustion studies (A.3.b.i) and laboratory discoveries including identification of gases (A.3.b.ii).
Dalton's atomic theory covers the development of atomic theory of matter (A.2.d.iv) and theories of molecular structure (A.3.c.iv), fundamental to the nature of chemistry (D.1).
Kepler's three laws of planetary motion directly address the heliocentric world system (A.1.c.i) and the discovery of laws of planetary motion (A.1.c.ii).
The planets of our solar system and exoplanet discoveries address major advances in solar-system astronomy (A.1.f.v) and component disciplines of astronomy (B.2).
The Sun's physics, nuclear fusion, and solar cycle address theories of stellar structure (A.1.f.ii) and astrophysics (B.5).
The vacuum of space from Democritus through Boyle to quantum field theory covers Greek physics (A.2.a), the search for the ether (A.2.d.i), and quantum theory (A.2.e.ii).
Antimatter from Dirac's prediction to baryon asymmetry addresses quantum theory development (A.2.e.ii) and elementary particle theories (A.2.e.iii).
Newton's Laws of Motion are foundational for 16th-17th century physics (A.2.c) and mechanics as a core discipline of physics (C.2).
Cosmic rays' role in discovering subatomic particles (positron, muon, pion) contributes to the development of elementary particle theories (A.2.e.iii).

Eclipses from Babylonian saros cycles to Eddington's 1919 relativity test cover ancient astronomical prediction (A.1.a.i), astronomical tests of general relativity (A.1.f.iii), and distance measurement (B.3).
The cool universe and infrared/submillimeter astronomy address development of radio, infrared, and other astronomy (A.1.f.vi) and advances in instrumentation (A.1.f.viii).
Asteroids from their 19th-century discovery address the discovery of asteroids (A.1.e.i), solar system exploration (A.1.f.v), and component disciplines of astronomy (B.2).
The history of magnetism from Gilbert through Maxwell's electromagnetic theory covers development of electromagnetic theories (A.2.d.ii) and core physics disciplines (C.2).
The neutron's discovery by Chadwick and its significance for nuclear fission directly addresses theories concerning atomic structure and nuclear interactions (A.2.e.iii).
Saturn's ring system, moons, and Cassini-Huygens discoveries address major advances in solar-system astronomy (A.1.f.v) and component disciplines of astronomy (B.2).
Jupiter's Galilean satellites, the Juno mission, and Europa's subsurface ocean address solar-system astronomy (A.1.f.v) and planetary science (B.2).
Solar wind physics and its interaction with Earth's magnetosphere address solar system astronomy (A.1.f.v) and interdisciplinary fields of physics including geophysics (C.5).
The invention of radio from Maxwell's theory through Hertz and Marconi covers development of electromagnetic wave theories (A.2.d.ii) and modern developments in physics (A.2.e.v).
The Kuiper Belt, trans-Neptunian objects, and the New Horizons mission address major advances in solar-system astronomy (A.1.f.v) and component disciplines of astronomy (B.2).
The life cycle of stars from formation through death covers theories of stellar formation and evolution (A.1.f.ii) and astrophysics (B.5).
The music of the spheres from Pythagoras through Kepler's Harmonices Mundi traces early cosmologies (A.1.a.ii) and the discovery of laws of planetary motion (A.1.c.ii).
Al-Kindi's contributions to optics connect to medieval astronomy through Islamic contributions (A.1.b).
